en: >> It is and and even those highest moments of my career, anyone's career, it's like you enjoy them and you look back on them and celebrate them, but they are quick, right? zh: 我發現與他人分享這段旅程，才是讓我一直熱愛辛苦訓練的關鍵，即使到現在也是如此。因為訓練真的會讓人疲憊，你知道的，馬拉松訓練需要跑很多里程，有很多枯燥的磨練。有一段時間我大部分都是獨自完成的，但我覺得花時間去做一些妥協，讓自己能和其他人一起訓練，真的為我的跑步帶來了很多新鮮感和樂趣。

en: finding a way to see running is only upside, you know, and not fearing failure. For me, I was really crippled by the fear of failure in my it started really in high school but grew in college and early on as a pro to the point I was really struggling as a pro and you know here I'm being paid to win and to succeed and like I got last place at our US nationals one year you know
zh: 同時也要找到一種方式，把跑步看成只有好處，不要害怕失敗。對我來說，我曾經被對失敗的恐懼深深束縛，從高中開始，到大學和職業生涯初期越來越嚴重，到了一個程度，我作為職業選手真的非常掙扎。你知道的，我是被付錢來贏得比賽和取得成績的，但有一年我在美國全國錦標賽上跑了最後一名。

Like it was so fun and exciting and such a contrast to like how I struggled early in my pro career. My first marathon was a complete disaster. zh: 是的，確實如此。你知道，我很喜歡職業生涯中追逐美國紀錄的那段時期。那真的很有趣、很興奮，和我職業生涯初期的掙扎形成了鮮明的對比。我的第一場馬拉松完全是場災難。所以能走到可以去追逐馬拉松和半程馬拉松美國紀錄的位置，對我來說已經是巨大的飛躍了。

zh: 但那也伴隨著沉重的代價，對吧？因為當你有一個如此精確、如此無情的目標時，訓練中容不得任何差錯。我得說，在追求那些目標的時候，我並不是最快樂的自己，因為那是持續不斷的焦慮，對吧？不斷試圖控制所有可控的事情。我有四個孩子，我努力做好他們的好媽媽，也努力做好我丈夫的好妻子。如果你是單身，可以整天躺在床上看 Netflix 那就容易多了，但那不是我的生活。

en: >> Yeah, absolutely. What are some some common mistakes that you see

I don't have any caffeine on

speaker: Guest

en: my easy days usually. So some days I'm just trudging cuz like I ran a marathon in training the day before and did weights and crossrained and so like I'm really tired and like I really just want to listen to my body and let my adrenals really relax. Um, versus I would say,
zh: 所以有些日子我就是在拖著腳步跑，因為前一天我可能跑了一個馬拉松訓練量，還做了重訓和交叉訓練，所以我真的很累，我就是想傾聽身體的聲音，讓腎上腺好好休息。
speaker: Guest

en: yeah, recreational athletes like maybe they don't have a coach that's giving them the structure of those hard workouts and things and they kind of just want to keep pushing what they can do that day, you know. Um, I would say
zh: 相比之下，業餘運動員可能沒有教練為他們安排高強度訓練的結構，他們就是想一直推進當天能做到的極限。
speaker: Guest

en: additionally just making sure your shoe choices are like running in the right shoes for you, like getting fitted and having someone look at your foot strike because like she from your foot strike everything is upstream of that, right? it's going to affect your knees and your hips and everything. And so if you're in a neutral shoe when you need arch support um like or like you need a custom orthotic, you know, like that that's huge. Like that could save you so many injuries and stuff. And vice versa,
zh: 另外我要說的是，一定要確保選對跑鞋，穿適合自己的鞋子，去做專業的鞋款適配，讓人檢查你的落腳方式。因為從你的落腳方式開始，一切都是往上影響的，對吧？它會影響你的膝蓋、髖部等等。所以如果你需要足弓支撐卻穿了中性鞋，或者你需要客製化的矯正鞋墊，那影響真的很大，可以幫你避免很多傷痛。
speaker: Guest

en: like if you're being pushed out by a motion control shoe and um on on the outside of your foot and you're not getting contact the with the big toe knuckle. That's such a big focus of my form work is being able to get contact there for further upstream. Being able to turn on your glute. Um, yeah. So, I

en: would say just really making sure you're in the right shoes and investing. That's really the only thing you need for running, right? Is like good fresh shoes and like making sure you prioritize that investment.

en: >> Yeah, proper running shoes can absolutely make a difference. For sure. That's a that's a big one. speaker: Guest

en: my car and texted my agent. I was like, "Please get me into the London Marathon." Um and yeah, you can

en: Um, and yeah, so all all these factors like I but I was like I don't care like whatever like whatever the race is going to be like I'm doing it because it's my one shot at getting to race right now. en: groups for the women's um women's only start world record which was 216 then pacer for 218 and then a pacer for 225 and so the the American records was 21936 which you know the is right in between like drastically in between 225 was way too slow so I wanted to go out with the 218 pacer even though that felt faster than I felt capable of

en: um but she decided just to kind of tuck in with the 216 group and like she was a little inexperienced I think or I don't know but and she didn't even make it very long. en: like of dropping out and and but then you know I kind of just slapped myself in the moment and be and was like you are so lucky to have an opportunity to race right now like you're in the shape of your life you're in super shoes for the first time they feel incredible like I just kept like pushing into the ground harder and feeling them bounce back and um and so so yeah, I ended up talking myself through the race and and then telling myself girls were going to start coming back to me and they did. en: that was beautifully written and and described. Just just go hunt people down. And um so yeah,

en: whatever it takes for you to get your mind in a better spot like stop look. you don't have to throw away your watch, but like maybe stop looking at it, you know, so that you're not seeing a split that sends you um mentally in a negative spot and like tune into the feeling of being out there, you know? zh: 所以，不管什麼方法能讓你的心態好轉——你不用把手錶丟掉，但也許可以不要一直看，這樣你就不會看到某個配速數字然後陷入負面情緒。去感受跑步本身的感覺。Ryan 也常常鼓勵我這樣做，尤其是現在我在變老。很長一段時間裡，每次馬拉松備戰我都越來越快，一直到 39 歲都在刷新個人紀錄，但我從 38 歲之後就再也沒有 PR 過了。

speaker: Guest

en: time. And another thing that just helped me was my faith. en: bothers you cuz I feel like it's when you when you feel like each race is high stakes like love and belonging is on the time on the line every time out when you line up that's when anxiety like performance anxiety starts and when failure really is hard because you're trying to prove yourself and your identity or you're trying to like um maintain that love and belonging. zh: 因為我覺得，當你覺得每場比賽都事關重大，每次站上起跑線時愛與歸屬感都懸在線上，那就是表現焦慮開始的時候，也是失敗真正讓人痛苦的時候，因為你在試圖證明自己的身份，或者試圖維持那份愛與歸屬感。
